counterevidenceSought: Checked transaction-local selection, lower delete predicates, caller serialization, and update/rescue race coverage.
alternativeExplanation: Same-process JavaScript cannot interleave without an await, but file-backed/multi-process writers remain possible.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if a process-wide writer lock or transaction-local expiry predicate is proven.

focus: auto-select + factory + registry provider precedence and fallback dimension handling

graphEvents.label: P1 non-Ollama active provider ignored by startup resolver

reviewDepthApplicability.reason: Provider selection spans bootstrap persistence, factory resolution, startup validation, registry fallbacks, vector shard attachment, and save/cache consumers.

targetSelection.selectionReason: Prompt-selected EMB-1/provider precedence and fallback dimension path; targets were chosen along the bootstrap selected-provider flow, then the later factory/startup resolver and first consumers of pre-init model/dimension.

findingDetails.title: Startup resolver ignores persisted non-Ollama active embedders, so local-first bootstrap can be overwritten by stale cloud config
findingDetails.claim: ensureActiveEmbedder can persist hf-local, OpenAI, or Voyage from the local-first bootstrap cascade, but the later factory startup resolver only recognizes persisted Ollama metadata. If hf-local wins bootstrap while a stale usable VOYAGE_API_KEY or OPENAI_API_KEY is present, startup validation and EMBEDDING_DIM can switch the process back to the cloud provider/dimension even though the active shard was attached for the persisted local profile.
findingDetails.counterevidenceSought: Checked whether resolveProvider reads generic active metadata, whether getProviderInfo reuses the bootstrap metadata after non-Ollama persistence, and whether tests cover hf-local selected while a cloud key exists. The generic reader exists only for hf-local dimension drift warnings, getProviderInfo calls resolveProvider again, and tests cover hf-local auto-selection plus no-key Ollama recovery but not stale cloud env after non-Ollama persistence.
findingDetails.alternativeExplanation: Operators may treat any usable cloud key as intentional. That does not fit the local-first bootstrap contract, because startup has already persisted and attached the selected active profile before the cloud validation/dimension path runs.
findingDetails.downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if startup is changed so resolveProvider consumes persisted active_embedder_provider for all provider kinds, or if EMBEDDING_DIM and pre-init model/dimension readers are proven unreachable after a non-Ollama active pointer is persisted.

searchLedger.hypothesis: The prior EMB-1 explicit-provider override issue may have a new, separately reportable branch.

searchActions.result: Startup still initializes and attaches active embedder before provider config validation.
Auto-select remains local-first while factory explicit provider resolution still gives EMBEDDINGS_PROVIDER precedence.
Explicit provider override bootstrap is already registered as DR-001-P1-001 / EMB-1.

searchLedger.rationale: The explicit override branch remains active but is already captured; this iteration does not re-report it as new.

searchLedger.hypothesis: A non-Ollama active pointer persisted by local-first bootstrap can be ignored by the later factory resolver, causing provider/dimension drift under partial-provider availability.

searchActions.result: ensureActiveEmbedder persists whichever auto-selected provider was selected, including hf-local/openai/voyage.
resolveProvider only considers readActiveOllamaEmbedder; that path rejects non-Ollama names via getOllamaManifest, while the generic active metadata reader is not part of provider selection.
Startup attaches active shard before validation, then sets EMBEDDING_DIM from resolveStartupEmbeddingConfig/getStartupEmbeddingDimension; pre-init getEmbeddingDimension reads that factory path.
Save/cache path reads model/dimension before generating, so wrong pre-init dimension can affect profile/cache lookup.

searchLedger.rationale: The selected active provider is not a single source of truth across bootstrap, factory startup, and dimension consumers.

searchLedger.invariant: Factory fallbacks should report dimension drift when a created provider falls back to a different dimension.

findingDetails.title: Step 11.5 daemon guard ignores live childPid in a stale launcher lease
findingDetails.claim: isSpecMemoryDaemonAlive only checks the launcher lease pid/ownerPid. The launcher lease also records childPid for the context-server daemon, but Step 11.5 ignores it, so a hard-killed launcher can leave a live context-server child holding the daemon database while standalone save concludes daemonStatus.alive=false and starts reindexSpecDocs as a second writer.
findingDetails.counterevidenceSought: Checked the launcher normal shutdown paths and tests. Signal handlers kill child processes and clear the lease, uncaughtException clears the lease, and current daemon-detect tests cover live pid, dead pid, missing lease, and canonical lease path. I did not find a test where lease.pid is dead but lease.childPid is live, nor logic that checks childPid before allowing Step 11.5 standalone indexing.
findingDetails.alternativeExplanation: A clean launcher shutdown should clear the lease and stop the child, and same-process runs are serialized by the workflow queue. The gap is the hard-death/stale-lease case: SIGKILL or process death before cleanup can leave the spawned context-server reparented while the lease still contains the live childPid that the detector ignores.
findingDetails.downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if the context-server child is proven unable to survive launcher hard death on supported runtimes, or if Step 11.5 is otherwise protected by an exclusive writer lease that covers the live child process before reindexSpecDocs runs.

Launcher lease payload includes childPid when context-server is spawned, but the detector ignores that field.

findingDetails.title: embedder_status drops live model-server load timestamps because the shared metadata contract uses the wrong type
findingDetails.claim: The local model server publishes loadStartedAt/loadProgressAt as millisecond numbers and the bridge probe consumes them that way, but HfLocalProvider only accepts string timestamps and ProviderMetadata types them as string|null. During a live loading/ready health check, applyHealthMetadata ignores the numeric fields, so embedder_status serializes null progress fields even though the server health payload contains the data.
findingDetails.counterevidenceSought: Checked whether the server emits ISO strings instead of numbers, whether the bridge treats strings as canonical, whether embedder_status bypasses HfLocalProvider metadata, and whether tests exercise the live server payload rather than a mocked provider. The server tests assert numbers, the bridge uses Number.isFinite on the fields, and embedder_status only returns HfLocalProvider.getMetadata after healthCheck.
findingDetails.alternativeExplanation: The strings in embedder-status.vitest could represent the intended public MCP shape, but that is not what the committed live model server emits, and no conversion layer exists between /api/health and ProviderMetadata.
findingDetails.downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if loadStartedAt/loadProgressAt are explicitly non-contractual for embedder_status consumers, or if another layer not reviewed here converts the live numeric health payload into strings before HfLocalProvider.applyHealthMetadata runs.

searchLedger.hypothesis: embedder_status modelServer progress fields can be present in /api/health but absent from the returned MCP payload because producer and metadata contract disagree on timestamp type.

The bridge probe treats loadProgressAt/loadStartedAt as finite millisecond numbers when deciding whether loading is wedged.
ProviderMetadata declares loadStartedAt/loadProgressAt as string|null, and applyHealthMetadata only stores them when the health payload fields are strings or null.
The embedder_status test uses mocked ISO strings, while the live model-server tests assert numeric timestamps, so no test covers the real integration contract.

searchLedger.rationale: The same health fields are numeric in both producer and bridge consumer, but the MCP status path silently filters them out by type before serializing modelServer metadata.
